perturbation.original_text: "Force Majeure" means acts of God, fires, floods, storms; compliance with orders of courts or Governmental Authorities; explosions, wars, terrorist acts, riots, strikes, lockouts or other industrial disturbances; accidental disruption of service; breakdown of machinery, storage tanks or pipelines and inability to obtain or unavoidable delays in obtaining material or equipment; and similar events or circumstances, so long as such events or circumstances are beyond the affected Party's reasonable control and could not have been prevented by the affected Party's due diligence; provided, however, that a Party's failure to pay any amounts due hereunder shall not constitute an event of Force Majeure.
perturbation.changed_text: "Force Majeure" means acts of God, fires, floods, storms; compliance with orders of courts or Governmental Authorities; explosions, wars, terrorist acts, riots, strikes, lockouts or other industrial disturbances; accidental disruption of service; breakdown of machinery, storage tanks or pipelines and inability to obtain or unavoidable delays in obtaining material or equipment; and similar events or circumstances, regardless of whether such events or circumstances are within the affected Party's reasonable control and even if the affected Party could have prevented it by the affected Party's due diligence; provided, however, that a Party's failure to pay any amounts due hereunder shall not constitute an event of Force Majeure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'beyond the affected Party's reasonable control and could not have been prevented by the affected Party's due diligence' clause, the definition of Force Majeure is significantly broadened. This creates uncertainty and contradicts the typical legal understanding of force majeure, which requires unforeseeability and uncontrollability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Force Majeure
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the requirements that a force majeure event must be beyond the party's control and not preventable with due diligence.  This contradicts the fundamental principle of force majeure, which typically requires that the event be unforeseeable and unavoidable to excuse performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: force majeure | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you force majeure Force majeure is a provision in a contract that frees both parties from obligation if an extraordinary event directly prevents one or both parties from performing. A non-performing party may use a force majeure clause as excuse for non-performance for circumstances beyond the party's control and not due to any fault or negligence by the non-performing party. However, mere impracticality or unanticipated difficulty is not enough to excuse performance. Indeed, courts generally do not recognize economic downturn as a force majeure event. This is because economic hardships occur regularly in business , and as a result, may be appropriately and preemptively dealt with by allocating its risk through the terms of the contract. As such, force majeure events are often labeled as " acts of god " and include both natural and man-made events like fires, floods, storms, war, and labor disputes. Some jurisdictions, like New York, interpret force majeure clauses narrowly and only grant excuses if the specific event is stated in the clause. See: Kel Kim Corp. v. Central Markets, Inc . As such, parties may agree to broaden or narrow the terms and conditions of performance. Still, when interpreting force majeure clauses, courts interpret them based on the parties' circumstances and refuse to enforce overtly broad force majeure clauses. For example, New York courts have recognized the COVID-19 pandemic and the resulting bar on non-essential business activity as a sufficient force majeure event to excuse performance where the term "natural disaster" was expressly stated as a circumstance that would trigger the clause. See: JN Contemporary Art LLC v. Phillips Auctioneers LLC . On the other hand, courts have refused to extend force majeure clauses (even those related to COVID-19-related hardships) where the underlying clause was written to cover any unforeseen or uncontrollable change, and where there was doubt to whether the event (as opposed to economic considerations) directly prohibited the non-performing party's nonperformance. See: Rudolph v. United Airlines Holdings, Inc. Lastly, force majeure clauses are among a number of defenses that can be asserted in response to an action for nonperformance, such as the general defense of impossibility and frustration of purpose . [Last reviewed in May of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of "Force Majeure" by removing the requirement that events must be beyond a party's control and preventable with due diligence. This contradicts the general legal understanding of force majeure, which requires unforeseeability and uncontrollability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a force majeure clause excuses non-performance for circumstances beyond the party's control and not due to any fault or negligence by the non-performing party. It also notes that courts interpret these clauses based on the parties' circumstances and may refuse to enforce overly broad clauses. By removing the 'control' and 'due diligence' elements, the contract creates an overly broad force majeure definition that could be deemed unenforceable, directly contradicting the intent of force majeure as generally understood in law.

perturbation.original_text: "Product" means the commodities commonly associated as specialty petroleum products, specifically condensate, natural gasoline, and diluent. Product may also include liquefied petroleum gas commodities such as butane. Product, as designed in this Agreement, does not refer to finished gasoline and diesel products.
perturbation.changed_text: "Product" means any substance that can be transported via pipeline, and is subject only to the discretion of MPL. For the avoidance of doubt, this includes hazardous waste, finished gasoline and diesel products, and any other materials MPL deems suitable for transportation. MPL may at its discretion limit the types of materials it transports from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition confines 'Product' to specific petroleum products. The modified version broadens it to include any substance suitable for pipeline transportation as determined by MPL, explicitly encompassing items like hazardous waste and finished gasoline, which have specific regulatory frameworks. This creates a conflict with laws governing the transportation of hazardous materials.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 49 U.S. Code § 60102 - Standards for pipeline facilities
perturbation.law_explanation: The revised definition allows for the transport of 'any substance', including hazardous waste and finished gasoline, without adhering to the stringent safety and environmental standards mandated by federal law for the transportation of hazardous materials via pipelines.  Specifically, 49 U.S.C. § 60102 requires the establishment of minimum safety standards, which are not accounted for in the contract's altered definition.

Purpose and general authority (a) Purpose and Minimum Safety Standards.- (1) Purpose .-The purpose of this chapter is to provide adequate protection against risks to life and property posed by pipeline transportation and pipeline facilities by improving the regulatory and enforcement authority of the Secretary of Transportation. (2) Minimum safety standards .-The Secretary shall prescribe minimum safety standards for pipeline transpo
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the definition of 'Product' to include 'any substance' suitable for pipeline transportation, which encompasses hazardous waste and finished gasoline. This contradicts 49 U.S.C. § 60102, which mandates the Secretary of Transportation to prescribe minimum safety standards for pipeline transportation to protect against risks to life and property. The revised definition's allowance for transporting 'any substance' without adherence to safety standards directly undermines the purpose and authority outlined in the law, creating a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.4 Shipper agrees to pay MPL monthly: (a) the Tariff Rates in effect for all Shipper Deliveries transported by MPL on the Pipeline during such month; and (b) any loading, handling, transfer and other charges incurred with respect to such Shipper Deliveries for such month in accordance with the provisions as set forth in the Tariffs (or any other tariffs that may be applicable to such Shipper Deliveries). If the amount owed by Shipper is the subject of a good faith dispute, Shipper shall be obligated to pay only the undisputed portion of such amount pending the resolution of such dispute in accordance with this Agreement. Late payments of undisputed amounts shall accrue interest at a rate equal to two percent (2%) per annum, until paid. Such payments will be paid by Shipper to MPL within fifteen (15) Days of the invoice date or resolution of any dispute, if applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4 Shipper agrees to pay MPL monthly: (a) the Tariff Rates in effect for all Shipper Deliveries transported by MPL on the Pipeline during such month; and (b) any loading, handling, transfer and other charges incurred with respect to such Shipper Deliveries for such month in accordance with the provisions as set forth in the Tariffs (or any other tariffs that may be applicable to such Shipper Deliveries). If the amount owed by Shipper is the subject of any dispute, Shipper shall be obligated to pay the full amount pending the resolution of such dispute in accordance with this Agreement. Late payments of disputed amounts shall accrue interest at a rate equal to twenty-five percent (25%) per annum, until paid. Such payments will be paid by Shipper to MPL within five (5) Days of the invoice date or resolution of any dispute, if applicable.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows the shipper to pay only the undisputed portion of an amount owed if there is a good faith dispute. The modified clause mandates the shipper pay the full amount of any disputed charge while also increasing the interest on late payments of disputed amounts to an usurious twenty-five percent, and also reducing the payment window from fifteen days to five. This imposes undue financial strain on the shipper and is subject to regulatory requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text mandates payment of the full disputed amount and imposes a 25% annual interest rate on late payments. This interest rate may exceed the maximum rate allowed by Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01(A), which regulates interest rates and aims to prevent usury. A rate of 25% per annum may be considered usurious and unlawful, creating a contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1343.01 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1343.01 | Maximum rate of interest. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1343 Interest Effective: June 29, 1988 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 130 - 117th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) The parties to a bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing for the forbearance or payment of money at any future time, may stipulate therein for the payment of interest upon the amount thereof at any rate not exceeding eight per cent per annum payable annually, except as authorized in division (B) of this section. (B) Any party may agree to pay a rate of interest in excess of the maximum rate provided in division (A) of this section when: (1) The original amount of the principal indebtedness stipulated in the bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing exceeds one hundred thousand dollars; (2) The payment is to a broker or dealer registered under the "Securities Exchange Act of 1934," 48 Stat. 881, 15 U.S.C. 78A, as amended, for carrying a debit balance in an account for a customer if such debit balance is payable on demand and secured by stocks, bonds or other securities; (3) The instrument evidences a loan secured by a mortgage or deed of trust on real estate where the loan has been approved, insured, guaranteed, purchased, or for which an offer or commitment to insure, guarantee, or purchase has been received, in whole or in part, by the federal government or any agency or instrumentality thereof, the federal national mortgage association, the federal home loan mortgage corporation, or the farmers home administration, all of which is authorized pursuant to the "National Housing Act,"
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01(A) regarding maximum interest rates. The original contract stipulated interest at 2% per annum on late payments of undisputed amounts. The changed text increases this to 25% per annum on late payments of disputed amounts. The scraped law snippet 1 states that parties may stipulate for interest at a rate "not exceeding eight per cent per annum payable annually" unless certain conditions are met (such as principal indebtedness exceeding $100,000, which is not specified here). The Law Explanation states that this interest rate may exceed the maximum rate allowed, potentially creating usury. Therefore, the modified clause creates a contradiction as it imposes an interest rate that potentially violates the statutory limit, assuming none of the exceptions in division (B) of the statute apply.
